title: A busy market or an empty park: which is better for thinking?
summary: Argue whether you think more clearly in a lively, crowded place or in a quiet, empty one, using a specific example from your experience.
topic: Whether a busy market or a quiet park is a better place for thinking
prompt: Write 3 paragraphs (400–500 words) arguing whether you think more clearly in a busy, stimulating environment or in a quiet, empty one. Use a specific place as an example. Give one reason supporting your choice and address one reason why the other side might appeal to some people. Use the present perfect, conditionals, and opinion phrases.
hintStarters: In my experience, I have always thought more clearly when…
Although many people believe that silence is essential for thinking, I would argue that…
If I had to choose one environment to work through a difficult problem, it would be…

hintVocab.phrase: to lose your train of thought

hintVocab.meaningVi: sự rõ ràng trong tâm trí
tập trung vào một nhiệm vụ
bị mắc kẹt, không tiến được

hintVocab.phrase: to take a walk to clear your head
hintVocab.meaningVi: đi bộ để giải tỏa đầu óc

hintVocab.phrase: to be in the right frame of mind
hintVocab.meaningVi: ở trạng thái tâm lý phù hợp
tắt não, nghỉ ngơi tinh thần
cảm thấy vững vàng, bình tâm

sampleText:
Most people, when they need to think through something difficult, instinctively look for silence. This preference is so common that we treat it as a fact about thinking rather than a preference about comfort. They close the door, put in headphones, find a corner. This makes obvious sense — distractions interrupt the thread of thought and silence gives the mind room to work. I understand the logic, but I have found, over years of working in different places, that it does not apply to me. The environment in which I have consistently thought best is a busy market, or anything with a similar quality of ambient noise and movement.

The market I have in mind is one in Istanbul, near Karaköy, where I lived for eight months. By mid-morning it was already full — vendors calling out prices, the clatter of trolleys on uneven stone, the smell of bread from two stalls over. I used to sit at a small table outside a tea house at the edge of the market with a notebook, and I found that I could think there in a way that I could not at my desk. Something about the movement around me made my own thoughts feel more mobile — as though ideas could circulate more easily when I was not the only thing in motion. My current theory is that the noise was varied enough to prevent me from focusing on any single sound, and yet constant enough to fill the gap where distraction usually enters. When the room is silent, any small noise — a notification, a door — breaks the surface. In the market, there was no surface to break.

I recognise that this preference is not universal. There are people who have never been able to work in noise, who need the particular stillness of an early morning room or an empty park to do their best thinking. That kind of quiet can be genuinely useful for certain tasks: careful editing, mathematical reasoning, anything that requires slow re-reading. If I were working on something that demanded that level of precision, I might choose an empty park myself. What I am arguing is not that noise is better than silence in some absolute way, but that the assumption that silence is always necessary for thought deserves to be questioned. The best environment for thinking is not the quietest one — it is the one that matches the kind of thinking you are trying to do. Knowing that about yourself is, in a small way, its own kind of knowledge.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Present perfect with 'over' + time phrase for accumulated experience
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'I have found, over years of working in different places, that…' dùng hiện tại hoàn thành với 'over years of…' để diễn đạt một khám phá tích lũy qua thời gian dài. Cách dùng này nhấn mạnh rằng kết luận được rút ra từ nhiều trải nghiệm, không phải một lần duy nhất.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'I have found, over years of working in different places, that…' uses the present perfect with 'over years of…' to express a discovery built up across a long time. This stresses that the conclusion comes from repeated experience, not a single event.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Second conditional with 'were' for a hypothetical present
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'If I were working on something that demanded that level of precision, I might choose an empty park myself.' Dùng 'were' (không phải 'was') trong mệnh đề if của điều kiện loại 2 ở văn phong trang trọng là quy tắc chuẩn, đặc biệt với ngôi thứ nhất số ít.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'If I were working on something that demanded that level of precision, I might choose an empty park myself.' Using 'were' (not 'was') in the if-clause of a second conditional in formal writing is standard, especially in the first person singular.

sampleTranslationVi:
Hầu hết mọi người, khi cần suy nghĩ về điều gì đó khó khăn, bản năng tìm kiếm sự im lặng. Họ đóng cửa lại, cắm tai nghe, tìm một góc khuất. Điều này có lý rõ ràng — sự phân tâm làm gián đoạn mạch suy nghĩ và sự im lặng cho phép tâm trí có không gian để làm việc. Tôi hiểu logic đó, nhưng tôi đã nhận ra, qua nhiều năm làm việc ở những nơi khác nhau, rằng nó không áp dụng được cho tôi. Môi trường mà tôi đã nhất quán suy nghĩ tốt nhất là một khu chợ náo nhiệt, hoặc bất cứ thứ gì có chất lượng tiếng ồn xung quanh và chuyển động tương tự.

Khu chợ tôi nghĩ đến là một khu chợ ở Istanbul, gần Karaköy, nơi tôi sống trong tám tháng. Vào giữa buổi sáng, nó đã đầy người — người bán hàng rao giá, tiếng va đập của xe đẩy trên mặt đá lồi lõm, mùi bánh mì từ hai gian hàng bên cạnh. Tôi thường ngồi ở một chiếc bàn nhỏ bên ngoài quán trà ở rìa chợ với một cuốn sổ tay, và tôi nhận thấy rằng tôi có thể suy nghĩ ở đó theo cách mà tôi không thể làm được tại bàn làm việc của mình. Lý thuyết hiện tại của tôi là tiếng ồn đủ đa dạng để ngăn tôi tập trung vào bất kỳ âm thanh nào, nhưng lại đủ liên tục để lấp đầy khoảng trống nơi sự phân tâm thường xâm nhập. Khi căn phòng im lặng, bất kỳ tiếng động nhỏ nào — một thông báo, một cánh cửa — đều phá vỡ bề mặt. Ở chợ, không có bề mặt nào để phá vỡ.

Tôi nhận ra rằng sở thích này không phổ biến. Có những người chưa bao giờ có thể làm việc trong tiếng ồn, những người cần sự yên tĩnh đặc biệt của một căn phòng vào sáng sớm hay một công viên vắng vẻ để suy nghĩ tốt nhất. Loại im lặng đó có thể thực sự hữu ích cho một số nhiệm vụ nhất định: việc chỉnh sửa cẩn thận, tư duy toán học, bất cứ điều gì đòi hỏi việc đọc lại chậm rãi. Nếu tôi đang làm việc với điều gì đó đòi hỏi mức độ chính xác đó, tôi có thể tự chọn một công viên vắng. Điều tôi đang lập luận không phải là tiếng ồn tốt hơn sự im lặng theo một cách tuyệt đối nào đó, mà là giả định rằng sự im lặng luôn cần thiết cho tư duy xứng đáng được đặt câu hỏi. Môi trường tốt nhất cho tư duy không phải là môi trường yên tĩnh nhất — đó là môi trường phù hợp với loại tư duy bạn đang cố gắng thực hiện.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses the present perfect with 'over years of' as an experience phrase?
options: I found, over years of working, that noise suits me better.
I have been find, over years of working, that noise suits me better.
I have finded, over years of working, that noise suits me better.
I have found, over years of working, that noise suits me better.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Have found' is the correct present perfect. Simple past 'found' loses the connection to accumulated ongoing experience. 'Have been find' is not the present perfect progressive. 'Finded' is not the past participle of 'find' — it is 'found'.
mcQuestions.hint: The past participle of 'find' is 'found', not 'finded'.

options: The noise was varied enough to prevent me from focusing on any single sound.
The noise was varied enough to prevent me to focus on any single sound.
The noise was varied enough to prevent me focus on any single sound.
The noise was varied enough to prevent me focusing on any single sound.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Prevent + object + from + gerund' is the standard construction. 'Prevent me to focus' uses an infinitive, which is incorrect. 'Prevent me focus' drops both 'from' and the gerund form. 'Prevent me focusing' is informal/dialectal but not standard in written English.
mcQuestions.hint: The fixed pattern is 'prevent + object + from + -ing form'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses the second conditional correctly with 'were' for a formal hypothetical?
options: If I was working on something that demanded precision, I might choose differently.
If I were working on something that demanded precision, I might choose differently.
If I were working on something that demands precision, I might choose differently.
If I were working on something that demanded precision, I may choose differently.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Were' is the formal second conditional form for 'I'. 'Was' is informal/colloquial. The 'that' clause should stay in the past ('demanded') to match the hypothetical frame. 'May' is possible but 'might' is more appropriate for a more remote hypothetical in second conditional.
mcQuestions.hint: In formal second conditionals, use 'were' for 'I' and keep 'might' (not 'may') in the result.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'deserve' correctly with a passive infinitive?
options: The assumption deserves to be questioning.
The assumption deserves being questioned.
The assumption deserves to be questioned.
The assumption deserves question.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Deserve to be + past participle' is the standard passive infinitive construction. 'Deserves to be questioning' uses a progressive where a state is needed. 'Deserves being questioned' is grammatically possible but less standard. 'Deserves question' is missing the infinitive entirely.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Deserve' in formal writing is followed by 'to be + past participle' for passive meaning.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which phrase correctly expresses that a quality is sufficient to cause a result?
options: enough varied to prevent distraction
varied enough for prevent distraction
varied enough preventing distraction
varied enough to prevent distraction
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Adjective + enough + to + infinitive' is the standard pattern. 'Enough + adjective' reverses the standard order. 'For + base verb' and 'enough + -ing' are both ungrammatical in this structure.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Enough' follows the adjective and is followed by 'to + base verb'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'instinctively' as an adverb of manner?
options: Most people instinctively look for silence when they need to think.
Most people look instinctively for silence when they need to think.
Most people look for silence instinctively when they need to think.
Most people instinctive look for silence when they need to think.
mcQuestions.explanation: Adverbs of manner like 'instinctively' most naturally sit before the main verb. Options B and C are grammatically acceptable but place the adverb in a less natural position. Option D uses the adjective form 'instinctive' instead of the adverb.
mcQuestions.hint: Adverbs of manner usually sit between the subject and the main verb in neutral word order.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'although' correctly to concede a point before arguing against it?
options: Although many people need silence to think, but some work better in noise.
Although many people need silence to think, some work better in noise.
Although many people need silence to think. Some work better in noise.
Although many people needs silence to think, some work better in noise.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Although + clause, main clause' — no 'but' after the comma, no period mid-sentence splitting the concession from its consequence. 'Many people needs' has wrong subject-verb agreement.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Although' introduces a subordinate clause; 'but' is redundant and incorrect after the comma.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'what I am arguing' as a cleft structure?
options: What I am arguing is not that noise is better, but the assumption deserves to be questioned.
What I am arguing is not that noise is better, but questioning the assumption.
What I am arguing is not that noise is better, but that the assumption deserves to be questioned.
What I argue is not that noise is better, but that the assumption deserves to be questioned.
mcQuestions.explanation: The cleft 'What I am arguing is not X, but Y' needs parallel structure: 'not that…, but that…'. Dropping 'that' in the second clause breaks the parallel. Using a gerund phrase ('questioning') is not parallel with 'that clause'. 'What I argue' (present simple) is grammatical but less immediate than 'What I am arguing'.
mcQuestions.hint: In 'not X, but Y' structures, X and Y should be grammatically parallel — both 'that' clauses here.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'match' correctly in a relative clause?
options: The best environment is the one that match the kind of thinking you are doing.
The best environment is the one which matching the kind of thinking you are doing.
The best environment is the one matches the kind of thinking you are doing.
The best environment is the one that matches the kind of thinking you are doing.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'The one that matches' — the relative pronoun 'that' is followed by a third-person singular verb ('matches'). 'That match' misses the agreement. 'Which matching' uses a participle where a finite verb is needed. 'The one matches' without a relative pronoun is informal/non-standard.
mcQuestions.hint: In a relative clause after 'the one', use 'that' + third-person singular verb.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'used to' to describe a past habit?
options: I used to sit at a small table outside a tea house with a notebook.
I used to sat at a small table outside a tea house with a notebook.
I was used to sit at a small table outside a tea house with a notebook.
I use to sit at a small table outside a tea house with a notebook.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Used to + bare infinitive' describes a past habit. 'Used to sat' uses past tense after 'to'. 'Was used to + gerund' means 'was accustomed to'. 'Use to' (no 'd') is incorrect except in negative/question forms.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Used to' for past habits is followed by the bare infinitive.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'in some absolute way' as a manner phrase?
options: I am not arguing that noise is better in some absolutely way.
I am not arguing that noise is better in some absolute way.
I am not arguing that noise is better in some way absolute.
I am not arguing that noise is better on some absolute way.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'In some absolute way' uses the adjective 'absolute' before the noun 'way'. 'Absolutely' is an adverb and does not modify a noun. 'In some way absolute' reverses the adjective-noun order in an unnatural way. 'On some absolute way' uses the wrong preposition.
mcQuestions.hint: Adjectives come before nouns in English; the preposition for 'manner/method' is 'in'.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Have you ever noticed that you think differently depending on where you are — not just better or worse, but in a different direction altogether? What does that suggest about how environment shapes thought?
